focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

Nick stood at the counter with his coffee going cold. The light through his kitchen window was good. It was a December morning in Oakland. The rain had paused between systems, and the sun found a seam in the cloud deck and pushed through at a low angle that caught the top of the BART elevated track and the roofline of the auto body shop across the street and turned everything it touched briefly amber. His brain produced the assessment before he could stop it: atmospheric coherence, mid-range. It was consistent with a single dominant light source. The color temperature was warm but within plausible bounds for late-afternoon winter — except this was morning, and the angle was wrong for afternoon, which meant the assessment was wrong, which meant he was not only evaluating the actual sky outside his actual window but evaluating it badly.

He'd bought canvas: two stretched 24-by-36s from the art supply store on Telegraph, the cheap ones, pre-primed cotton that he would have bitched about three years ago but that now felt like an extravagance. They'd cost forty-six dollars for both. He'd also grabbed a tube of titanium white because his old one had dried to chalk in the cap, and a pint of odorless mineral spirits because the turpentine under the sink had gone thick and yellow. The rest of his paints were still usable, mostly — the cadmiums, the ultramarine, the raw umber, the alizarin crimson — sealed tight enough that the pigment hadn't separated beyond saving. He arranged them on the table like a sacrament. He hadn't set up to paint since August, maybe July. It had been four months. The canvases stacked against the far wall were the old work, the unsold landscapes from the Temescal show. He wasn't touching those. He set one of the new canvases on the easel an...
open_hooks_to_carry: Nick opening the batch and recognizing his own paintings — scraped, thumbnailed, stripped of his name
Tommy rating one of Nick's paintings a 2 for stylistic coherence — not even pausing
Bao rating another a 4, pausing over it, tilting his head
Nick rating his own work: he gives himself a 4 because a 5 would flag as outlier

payoffs_to_land.description: Nick's unsold paintings, whose online existence was seeded in Ch 1, appear in the training pipeline
The gallery near-miss in Ch 11 escalates to direct encounter — Nick's own work, not just his gallery's walls

prev_tail_excerpt:
...the one where he'd sold nothing. Not a single piece over the five weeks it was up, not even the small study of the Piedmont hills that he'd priced at eight hundred dollars, which was less than the materials and framing had cost. The gallery had kept the work up for an extra month out of some combination of stubbornness and pity, then moved it to the back room, then called him to come pick it up. He remembered backing the Civic into the alley behind the gallery. He remembered the bubble wrap the owner insisted on even for the short drive because she cared about work even when it didn't sell. He remembered carrying the canvases up three flights and leaning them against the wall of the apartment where they still stood.

The HVAC cycled. Lila typed something. Somewhere behind him Russ laughed once at whatever was in Slack and stopped.

Nick's hand stayed on the mouse. The rating fields sat empty at the bottom of the screen: leakage severity, source resemblance, overall quality. Above them, the little easel on the monitor held the words VERA LUX, and under that his own name, and under that the prices of paintings still leaning against his apartment wall.

Gallery sites never posted one room shot and stopped. They posted the whole show as a packet: installation views, wall crops, straight-on JPEGs of each painting, detail shots, the price PDF. If Canopy's archive had ingested the Vera Lux installation photo deeply enough for the audit tool to recover the source URL and for the model to reproduce the easel text, then the rest of the folder was already in the system. His landscapes were not a paranoid leap from this room. They were the next files in the packet.

He filled in the leakage fields and sent the batch on.

If the gallery was in the pipeline, the paintings were next.
state_deltas_required: Nick finds his own unsold paintings in the training data and must rate them alongside coworkers who don't know they're his
Identity crisis — the boundary between artist and system collapses; Nick is both the creator and the machine that digests creation
